close_reason: PVCAM Memory Pool Architecture Migration complete. All 9 sub-epics done: Foundation, Arrow Tap Removal, FrameData Integration, Frame Distribution Redesign, FrameProducer Trait, Consumer Updates, Validation. Pool achieves ~300x faster frame handling vs heap allocation.
description:
# PVCAM Memory Pool Architecture Migration

**Goal**: Migrate rust-daq's PVCAM driver from inefficient per-frame heap allocations (~16MB/frame) to a zero-allocation pool-based architecture modeled after the planmeca project.

## Problem Statement

The PVCAM driver currently allocates approximately 16 MB of heap memory per frame during high-speed acquisition:

| Allocation | Size | Location | Line |
|------------|------|----------|------|
| sdk_bytes.to_vec() | ~8 MB | acquisition.rs | 2789 |
| Arc::new(frame) | 64 bytes | acquisition.rs | 3000 |
| frame_arc.data.clone() for Arrow | ~8 MB | acquisition.rs | 3077 |
| **Total** | **~16 MB** | | |

At 100 FPS with 8 MB frames, this results in **800+ MB/second of allocation churn**.

## Root Cause

The problematic code at acquisition.rs:2786-2790:
- sdk_bytes.to_vec() allocates 8 MB every frame
- Arrow tap clones another 8 MB per frame

## Why This Matters

1. **Allocation latency** - malloc() introduces jitter in frame acquisition hot path
2. **Memory fragmentation** - Rapid large allocations fragment the heap
3. **Cache inefficiency** - Non-contiguous allocations cause cache misses
4. **Inconsistent with planmeca** - User's other project achieves zero allocations

## Solution Architecture

- Replace per-frame Vec allocation with pre-allocated Pool<FrameData>
- Replace broadcast channel with single-owner mpsc + tap system
- Remove Arrow tap feature entirely
- Port proven pool implementation from planmeca project

## Key Design Decisions (FINAL)

| Decision | Rationale |
|----------|-----------|
| Remove Arrow tap | User confirmed not needed |
| Breaking API changes OK | User is only consumer |
| Single primary consumer | Enables deterministic pool reclamation |
| Tap system for secondary | Flexibility without compromising pool |
| Port planmeca pool crate | Battle-tested implementation |

## Reference Files

- **Source pool**: /Users/briansquires/code/planmeca/crates/pool/src/lib.rs
- **Target**: crates/daq-driver-pvcam/src/components/acquisition.rs
- **Trait**: crates/daq-core/src/capabilities.rs

## Success Metrics

- **Before**: ~800 MB/sec allocation rate at 100 FPS
- **After**: ~0 allocations in frame acquisition hot path
- **Validation**: Hardware tests on maitai with Prime BSI camera

## Sub-Epics

1. Foundation - Pool Crate & Metrics (Phase 0-1)
2. Arrow Tap Removal (Phase 1.5)
3. FrameData & Pool Integration (Phase 2)
4. Frame Distribution Redesign (Phase 3)
5. FrameProducer Trait Migration (Phase 4)
6. Consumer Updates (Phase 5)
7. Validation & Performance (Phase 6)
title: PVCAM Memory Pool Architecture Migration

description:
# Sub-Epic 1: Foundation - Pool Crate & Allocation Metrics

**Phase**: 0-1 (First to implement)
**Parent Epic**: PVCAM Memory Pool Architecture Migration

## Overview

This sub-epic establishes the foundation for the memory pool migration:
1. Add allocation tracking instrumentation to measure before/after
2. Port the proven Pool<T> implementation from planmeca
3. Create the daq-pool crate in the rust-daq workspace

## Why Metrics First

Before making any changes, we need baseline measurements:
- Current allocations per frame
- Current allocation rate (MB/sec)
- Frame acquisition latency distribution

This allows us to validate the migration succeeded.

## Reference Implementation

**Source**: /Users/briansquires/code/planmeca/crates/pool/src/lib.rs

The planmeca pool provides:
- Pool<T> with semaphore + lock-free SegQueue for O(1) acquire/release
- Loaned<T> RAII guard with automatic return on drop
- Dynamic growth capability with error logging (backpressure indicator)
- try_acquire() for non-blocking acquisition
- Comprehensive test coverage (15 test functions)

## Key Files to Create

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| crates/daq-pool/Cargo.toml | Pool crate manifest |
| crates/daq-pool/src/lib.rs | Pool<T> and Loaned<T> implementation |
| Cargo.toml (workspace) | Add daq-pool to workspace members |

## Dependencies for daq-pool Crate

```toml
[dependencies]
crossbeam-queue = "0.3"  # For SegQueue (lock-free MPMC queue)
parking_lot = "0.12"      # For RwLock (faster than std)
tokio = { version = "1", features = ["sync"] }  # For Semaphore
log = "0.4"               # For growth warning logs
```

## Pool Design Notes

The planmeca pool uses UnsafeCell internally but is safe because:
1. Semaphore ensures at most N permits outstanding
2. Each permit corresponds to exactly one slot index
3. SegQueue ensures each index held by at most one Loaned
4. Loaned provides &T/&mut T with proper borrow checker enforcement

## Deliverables

1. Allocation tracking instrumentation added to acquisition.rs
2. daq-pool crate created and added to workspace
3. Pool<T> and Loaned<T> types fully implemented
4. Unit tests passing
5. Baseline allocation metrics documented

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Can measure allocations per frame in existing code
- [ ] daq-pool crate builds successfully
- [ ] All pool unit tests pass
- [ ] Pool can be used from daq-driver-pvcam crate
title: Sub-Epic 1: Foundation - Pool Crate & Allocation Metrics

description:
# Port Pool<T> Implementation from planmeca

**Purpose**: Copy the proven pool implementation from planmeca project.

## Source File

```
/Users/briansquires/code/planmeca/crates/pool/src/lib.rs
```

## Key Components to Port

### 1. Pool<T> Struct (lines 49-65)

```rust
pub struct Pool<T> {
    slots: RwLock<Vec<UnsafeCell<T>>>,
    free_indices: SegQueue<usize>,
    semaphore: Semaphore,
    reset_fn: Option<ResetFn<T>>,
    factory: FactoryFn<T>,
    initial_size: usize,
    current_size: AtomicUsize,
}
```

### 2. Safety Markers (lines 67-74)

```rust
unsafe impl<T: Send> Send for Pool<T> {}
unsafe impl<T: Send> Sync for Pool<T> {}
```

### 3. Pool Methods (lines 76-290)

- `new<F, R>()` - Constructor with optional reset function
- `new_simple<F>()` - Constructor without reset
- `new_with_reset<F, R>()` - Constructor with reset
- `grow()` - Dynamic growth (logs error for backpressure)
- `acquire()` - Async blocking acquire
- `try_acquire()` - Non-blocking acquire
- `acquire_or_grow()` - Acquire with auto-growth
- `release()` - Return slot to pool
- `size()`, `available()`, `initial_size()` - Getters

## Required Modifications

### 1. Use tracing instead of log

```rust
// Change from:
log::error!("Pool<{}> exhausted! ...", ...);

// To:
tracing::error!(
    target: "daq_pool",
    pool_type = std::any::type_name::<T>(),
    old_size = old_size,
    new_size = new_size,
    "Pool exhausted, growing"
);
```

### 2. Update Dependencies

planmeca uses `log`, we prefer `tracing`:

```toml
[dependencies]
tracing = "0.1"  # Instead of log
```

### 3. Add Module Documentation

Update doc comments to reference rust-daq context rather than planmeca.

## Test by Compiling

```bash
cargo build -p daq-pool
cargo test -p daq-pool
```

## Files to Modify

- crates/daq-pool/src/lib.rs - Port Pool<T> implementation
- crates/daq-pool/Cargo.toml - May need to adjust deps

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Pool<T> struct ported with all fields
- [ ] Send+Sync implementations ported with safety comments
- [ ] All Pool methods ported and compile
- [ ] log calls converted to tracing
- [ ] Documentation updated for rust-daq context
- [ ] cargo build -p daq-pool succeeds
title: Port Pool<T> implementation from planmeca

description:
# Sub-Epic 2: Arrow Tap Removal

**Phase**: 1.5 (Do early to simplify later phases)
**Parent Epic**: PVCAM Memory Pool Architecture Migration

## Overview

Remove the arrow_tap feature entirely. User confirmed this feature is not needed, and removing it:
1. Eliminates 8 MB/frame allocation from hot path
2. Simplifies Phase 3-5 significantly (fewer code paths)
3. Reduces maintenance burden

## Current Arrow Tap Implementation

The arrow_tap feature currently:
1. Clones frame data into Arrow Buffer (8 MB allocation)
2. Creates UInt16Array for downstream consumers
3. Sends via mpsc channel with try_send (non-blocking)

```rust
// acquisition.rs:3072-3081
#[cfg(feature = "arrow_tap")]
if let Some(ref tap) = arrow_tap {
    use arrow::array::{PrimitiveArray, UInt16Type};
    use arrow::buffer::Buffer;
    let buffer = Buffer::from(frame_arc.data.clone());  // 8 MB clone!
    let arr = Arc::new(PrimitiveArray::<UInt16Type>::new(Arc::new(buffer), None));
    let _ = tap.try_send(arr);
}
```

## Files to Modify

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| crates/daq-driver-pvcam/Cargo.toml | Remove arrow_tap feature and arrow dependency |
| crates/daq-driver-pvcam/src/lib.rs | Remove set_arrow_tap method and arrow_tap field |
| crates/daq-driver-pvcam/src/components/acquisition.rs | Remove arrow_tap from AcquisitionState, start_continuous_acquisition, frame loop |
| crates/daq-hardware/Cargo.toml | Remove pvcam_arrow feature |

## Files to Delete

| File | Reason |
|------|--------|
| crates/daq-driver-pvcam/examples/arrow_tap.rs | Example no longer valid |
| crates/daq-driver-pvcam/benches/arrow_tap.rs | Benchmark no longer valid |

## Code Locations to Clean Up

1. **AcquisitionState struct** (line ~940-941):
   ```rust
   #[cfg(feature = "arrow_tap")]
   pub arrow_tap: Arc<Mutex<Option<...>>>
   ```

2. **AcquisitionState::new()** (line ~1011-1012):
   ```rust
   #[cfg(feature = "arrow_tap")]
   arrow_tap: Arc::new(Mutex::new(None)),
   ```

3. **set_arrow_tap method** (line ~1250-1256)

4. **start_continuous_acquisition** (lines ~1316-1317, ~1388-1389, ~1799-1800, ~1850-1851)

5. **Frame loop** (lines ~2068, ~2377, ~3072-3081)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] arrow_tap feature removed from all Cargo.toml files
- [ ] All arrow_tap code removed from acquisition.rs
- [ ] set_arrow_tap method removed from lib.rs
- [ ] Example and benchmark files deleted
- [ ] Project builds without arrow_tap
- [ ] No references to arrow_tap remain in codebase
title: Sub-Epic 2: Arrow Tap Removal

description:
# Pool Sizing Strategy

## Problem
The plan doesn't specify how many frame slots to pre-allocate. This is critical because:
- Too few slots → frequent pool exhaustion → frame drops
- Too many slots → excessive memory usage (8MB × N)

## Sizing Analysis

### Memory Budget
| Pool Size | Memory Usage | Notes |
|-----------|--------------|-------|
| 10 slots  | 80 MB | Minimum viable |
| 20 slots  | 160 MB | Matches SDK buffer count |
| 30 slots  | 240 MB | Comfortable margin |
| 50 slots  | 400 MB | High headroom |
| 100 slots | 800 MB | Excessive |

### Latency Analysis
At 100 FPS (10ms per frame), pool must have enough slots to cover:
- Storage write latency: ~10-50ms per frame (HDD/SSD)
- GUI update latency: ~16ms (60 FPS display)
- Network latency (gRPC): ~10-100ms (variable)
- Pipeline stalls: occasional 200ms+ (GC, disk flush)

**Worst case pipeline stall**: 500ms at 100 FPS = 50 frames in flight

### Recommendation
**30 slots (240 MB)** as default:
- Covers 300ms of frames at 100 FPS
- Matches SDK's 20-slot buffer with 50% headroom
- Reasonable memory footprint

**Configurable via parameter**:
```rust
// In PvcamDriver parameters
Parameter::new("pool.frame_count", 30)
    .with_range(10, 100)
    .with_description("Number of pre-allocated frame slots")
```

## Implementation
1. Add `pool_size` parameter to driver config
2. Create pool with configured size at stream start
3. Log initial memory allocation
4. Track high-water mark during operation
5. Warn if pool utilization exceeds 80%

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Pool size is configurable (default: 30)
- [ ] Pool creation logs memory footprint
- [ ] Runtime metrics track pool utilization
- [ ] Documentation explains sizing tradeoffs
title: Define pool sizing strategy and configuration

Added two contention profiling tests that verify RwLock is not a bottleneck:
1. test_no_rwlock_contention_on_access: Concurrent access + acquire/release - 0% slow accesses
2. test_concurrent_readers_no_contention: Pure concurrent readers - max 12us latency

Both tests confirm the cached pointer optimization (slot_ptr) eliminates per-access locking. The pool design already caches slot pointers at acquire time, so get()/Deref does NOT take the RwLock.

**Purpose**: Create architecture decision record documenting the migration.

docs/architecture/adr-pool-migration-results.md

## Document Structure

```markdown
# ADR: PVCAM Memory Pool Migration Results

## Status
Accepted / Implemented

## Context
The PVCAM driver was allocating ~16 MB per frame (to_vec + Arrow clone),
resulting in 800+ MB/sec allocation churn at 100 FPS.

## Decision
Migrated to pool-based architecture modeled after planmeca project:
- Port planmeca's Pool<T>/Loaned<T> as daq-pool crate
- Replace broadcast with single-owner mpsc + tap system
- Remove Arrow tap feature

## Consequences

### Performance Results

| Metric | Before | After | Improvement |
|--------|--------|-------|-------------|
| Allocations/frame | ~2 | ~0 | ~100% reduction |
| Alloc bytes/frame | ~16 MB | ~0 | ~100% reduction |
| Frame latency P99 | X ms | Y ms | Z% reduction |

### Hardware Test Results

| Test | Duration | Result |
|------|----------|--------|
| Short burst | 30s | PASS |
| Medium run | 60s | PASS |
| Long run | 300s | PASS |
| Stress test | 60s | PASS |

### API Changes

The FrameProducer trait changed:
- Removed: \`subscribe_frames()\` (deprecated, returns None)
- Added: \`register_primary_output()\`
- Added: \`register_tap()\`
- Added: \`unregister_tap()\`

### Code Changes

| Component | Files Changed | Lines Added | Lines Removed |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|---------------|
| daq-pool | New crate | ~600 | 0 |
| PVCAM driver | Multiple | ~X | ~Y |
| Core traits | capabilities.rs | ~Z | ~W |

### Lessons Learned

1. ...
2. ...
3. ...

## References

- [Original plan document]

```

```
docs/guides/frame-api-migration.md
```

```markdown
# Frame API Migration Guide

## Why the API Changed

The old broadcast-based API (\`subscribe_frames()\`) prevented efficient
memory reuse because Arc<Frame> could be held by any subscriber indefinitely.

## Migration Steps

### Before (Old API)

\`\`\`rust
let rx = camera.subscribe_frames().await.unwrap();
camera.start_stream().await?;
while let Ok(frame) = rx.recv().await {
    process(&frame);
}
\`\`\`

### After (New API)

\`\`\`rust
// Primary consumer (owns frames)
let (tx, mut rx) = mpsc::channel(100);
camera.register_primary_output(tx).await?;
camera.start_stream().await?;
while let Some(frame) = rx.recv().await {
    process(&frame);
    // frame dropped here → pool slot returns
}
\`\`\`

### For Secondary Consumers (GUI, gRPC)

\`\`\`rust
// Use tap system
let (tx, mut rx) = mpsc::channel(10);
let handle = camera.register_tap(TapConsumer::Decimated {
    sender: tx,
    nth: 10,  // Every 10th frame
}).await?;

while let Some(bytes) = rx.recv().await {
    display(&bytes);
}

camera.unregister_tap(handle).await?;
\`\`\`

## Common Patterns

### Storage (Primary)
...

### GUI Preview (Decimated Tap)
...

### gRPC Streaming (Full Tap)
...
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] ADR created with all sections
- [ ] Performance results documented with real numbers
- [ ] Hardware test results documented
- [ ] API changes documented
- [ ] Migration guide created
- [ ] Examples for common patterns
- [ ] Lessons learned captured
title: Document migration results in ADR

close_reason: Created comprehensive ADR at docs/architecture/adr-pool-error-handling.md covering all failure modes, recovery strategies, metrics, and acceptance criteria
description:
# Error Handling Strategy

## Failure Modes & Responses

### 1. Pool Creation Failure
**Cause**: OOM during pre-allocation
```rust
let pool = FramePool::new(30, || FrameData::with_capacity(8_000_000), ...);
```
**Response**: Return error from start_stream(), don't start acquisition

### 2. Pool Exhaustion (try_acquire returns None)
**Cause**: Consumer backpressure
**Response**: 
- Drop the frame (don't copy to pool)
- Increment `frames_dropped_pool_exhaustion` counter
- Log warning (rate-limited to 1/second)
- Continue acquiring next frame

```rust
match frame_pool.try_acquire() {
    Some(slot) => { /* copy to slot */ }
    None => {
        pool_exhaustion_counter.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
        if should_log_warning() {
            tracing::warn!(
                "Pool exhausted, dropped {} frames", 
                pool_exhaustion_counter.load(Ordering::Relaxed)
            );
        }
        continue;  // Skip to next frame
    }
}
```

### 3. Primary Channel Full (try_send returns error)
**Cause**: Storage writer too slow
**Response**:
- Keep the Loaned frame (it goes back to pool on drop)
- Increment `frames_dropped_channel_full` counter
- Log warning (rate-limited)

```rust
if primary_tx.try_send(frame).is_err() {
    channel_full_counter.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
    // frame is automatically returned to pool when dropped here
}
```

### 4. Primary Channel Closed (receiver dropped)
**Cause**: Consumer task crashed or was stopped
**Response**:
- Log error
- Set flag to stop acquisition gracefully
- Equivalent to "no subscribers" timeout

### 5. Tap Channel Full
**Cause**: GUI/gRPC too slow
**Response**:
- Drop tap update (expected behavior)
- No logging (taps are best-effort)

## Metrics to Track

```rust
pub struct PoolMetrics {
    /// Frames dropped due to pool exhaustion
    pub frames_dropped_pool_exhaustion: AtomicU64,
    /// Frames dropped due to primary channel full
    pub frames_dropped_channel_full: AtomicU64,
    /// Frames dropped due to tap channel full (per tap)
    pub frames_dropped_tap: AtomicU64,
    /// High water mark of pool utilization
    pub pool_high_water_mark: AtomicUsize,
    /// Current pool utilization
    pub pool_current_usage: AtomicUsize,
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All failure modes have documented responses
- [ ] Pool exhaustion doesn't panic or corrupt state
- [ ] Channel closure is handled gracefully
- [ ] Metrics track all drop types
- [ ] Warnings are rate-limited to prevent log spam
title: Define error handling strategy for pool operations

Plan includes: (1) Runtime feature toggle via PVCAM_USE_POOL=0 env var, (2) Git revert strategy with preserved original code paths, (3) Five validation checkpoints with pass/fail criteria, (4) Symptoms requiring rollback and detection methods, (5) Execution checklist for both runtime and code rollbacks.
description:
# Rollback Plan

## Why We Need This
This is a significant architectural change to a critical code path. If the new pool-based implementation has unforeseen issues (performance regressions, subtle bugs, timing problems), we need a clear path back to the working state.

## Rollback Strategy

### Git Branch Strategy
```
main
  └── feat/pvcam-pool-migration   (new work)
```

- All changes on feature branch
- No merges to main until validation complete
- Keep original code intact with feature flag

### Feature Flag for Gradual Rollout
```rust
// In acquisition.rs
let use_pool = std::env::var("PVCAM_USE_POOL")
    .map(|v| v == "1")
    .unwrap_or(false);

if use_pool {
    // New pool-based path
    acquire_with_pool(frame_ptr, frame_bytes, &frame_pool, &primary_tx).await;
} else {
    // Original to_vec() path (unchanged)
    let pixel_data = unsafe {
        std::slice::from_raw_parts(frame_ptr as *const u8, copy_bytes).to_vec()
    };
    // ... existing broadcast code ...
}
```

### Validation Checkpoints

| Checkpoint | Pass Criteria | Rollback Trigger |
|------------|--------------|------------------|
| Unit tests | 100% pass | Any failure |
| Mock integration | 100% pass | Any failure |
| Hardware 200 frames | 0 drops at 100 FPS | >1% drop rate |
| 24h soak test | Stable memory, no leaks | Memory growth |
| Production pilot | No regressions | Any issue |

### Rollback Execution
If rollback needed at any checkpoint:

1. **During development**: `git checkout main` and continue with original code
2. **After merge**: Revert commit or disable via `PVCAM_USE_POOL=0`
3. **In production**: Environment variable flip, no code change needed

## What NOT to Delete
Until validation complete, keep:
- [ ] Original `subscribe_frames()` implementation
- [ ] Original broadcast channel setup
- [ ] Original `Frame::from_bytes()` path
- [ ] Arrow tap code (already planned for removal, but keep until pool is proven)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Feature flag controls pool usage
- [ ] Original code path preserved and functional
- [ ] Rollback documented in ADR
- [ ] Team knows rollback procedure
title: Define rollback plan if pool migration fails

close_reason: Analysis determined that the effort-to-benefit ratio is poor. The current daq-pool implementation is purpose-built for PVCAM frame capture with features (async timeout, auto-growth, reset-on-return) that sharded-slab doesn't provide natively. Migration would require ~400-500 lines of glue code, reducing net savings to only ~300 lines. The existing unsafe code is well-contained, documented, and tested. Engineering time better spent on documentation and integration tests.
description:
Investigate whether the custom object pool implementation in daq-pool can be replaced with the battle-tested `sharded-slab` crate to reduce maintenance burden and eliminate ~800 lines of unsafe code.

## Background

The current daq-pool implementation (~1,200 lines) provides:
- Semaphore + SegQueue pattern for lock-free access
- Cached pointer in `Loaned<T>` for lock-free access after acquire
- Auto-growth with backpressure logging
- Reset function on return

The `sharded-slab` crate (used by `tracing-subscriber`, 10M+ downloads/month) provides:
- Lock-free sharded slab allocation
- Similar pooling semantics via `sharded_slab::Pool`
- Production-tested in Tokio ecosystem

## Key Questions

1. Can `sharded-slab::Pool` match the `try_acquire_timeout` async semantics needed for PVCAM frame capture?
2. Does `sharded_slab::Clear` trait provide sufficient reset-on-return functionality?
3. What's the performance difference in the hot path (PVCAM callback thread)?

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Create prototype branch with sharded-slab replacement
- [ ] Benchmark PVCAM frame capture path (acquire latency, throughput)
- [ ] Document any API gaps that require wrappers
- [ ] Make go/no-go decision based on benchmarks
design:
## Approach

1. Study sharded-slab API (especially `Pool::create_with`, `Pool::get`, `Clear` trait)
2. Create minimal prototype replacing Pool<T> with sharded_slab::Pool<T>
3. Adapt Loaned<T> to use sharded_slab::pool::Ref/RefMut
4. Handle reset semantics via Clear trait implementation
5. For async timeout: may need wrapper with tokio::time::timeout around blocking acquire

## Key Files

- `crates/daq-pool/src/lib.rs` - Main pool implementation
- `crates/daq-pool/src/buffer_pool.rs` - BufferPool specialization
- `crates/daq-driver-pvcam/src/pvcam_loop.rs` - Primary consumer

## Risk Assessment

- MEDIUM: sharded-slab doesn't have native async timeout
- LOW: API adaptation should be straightforward
- LOW: Reset semantics via Clear trait
title: Evaluate replacing daq-pool with sharded-slab

notes:
## Problem
Hardcoded "data" path in storage modules:
- Default data directory is not configurable
- Different deployments need different paths
- No standard location for temporary vs permanent storage

## Current State
Storage paths are hardcoded or use relative paths like "data/".

## Solution
1. Add `data_path` to configuration file
2. Support environment variable `DAQ_DATA_PATH`
3. Provide sensible defaults per platform:
   - Linux: `/var/lib/daq/data` or `~/.local/share/daq/data`
   - macOS: `~/Library/Application Support/daq/data`
4. CLI flag override: `--data-path`

## Priority Order
1. CLI flag
2. Environment variable
3. Config file
4. Platform default

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Configuration option added to config schema
- [ ] Environment variable support (DAQ_DATA_PATH)
- [ ] CLI flag added to daemon
- [ ] Platform-appropriate defaults
- [ ] Documentation updated

close_reason: Implemented, tested (58/58 pass), reviewed by 3-model panel (G3 Pro, Codex, Opus). Review fixes applied.
description: HDF5Writer run_loop() is an infinite interval with no shutdown signal. When the task is cancelled (handle.abort()), up to 1s of data can be lost. Implement: (1) CancellationToken/watch channel to exit loop gracefully, (2) shutdown_and_join() method that signals stop + does final flush + waits for task to exit, (3) main.rs calls writer.shutdown_and_join() instead of abort(). Depends on RingBuffer wait_drained() from bd-qa36.6 for drain detection. Design: flush → wait_drained(5s) → flush again → close. Source: hdf5_writer.rs:196 (run_loop), main.rs:417 (handle.abort). Flagged by all 3 models (G3 Pro, Codex, Opus).
title: Add HDF5Writer graceful shutdown (shutdown_and_join pattern)

close_reason: Closed
description: The 6-phase shutdown state machine requires an 'Abort' phase to cancel running scripts/plans BEFORE cutting off hardware. Currently start_server_with_hardware() does not return a handle to ScriptEngine or RunEngine. Need to: (1) modify server startup to accept/return a CancellationToken or ServerHandle, (2) DaemonManager can trigger script abort before hardware shutdown, (3) prevents scripts from operating on disconnected hardware. Gap identified by G3 Pro: without this, Phase 2 'Abort' of the 6-phase shutdown cannot be implemented.
title: Expose RunEngine control surface for script abort during shutdown

close_reason: Implemented, tested (58/58 pass), reviewed by 3-model panel (G3 Pro, Codex, Opus). Review fixes applied.
description:
Create RAII SafetySentinel with these specifics (consensus from G3 Pro + Codex):
struct SafetySentinel { registry: Arc<DeviceRegistry>, armed: AtomicBool }
- Instantiate IMMEDIATELY after DeviceRegistry creation, before any startup logic
- In Drop: if armed.load(SeqCst), spawn emergency shutdown task
- Disarm ONLY after successful completion of shutdown Phase 6 (Disconnect)
- Covers gaps panic hook misses: early returns, Result unwinding, deadlocks
- G3 Pro: Sentinel should also integrate with systemd sd_notify watchdog — if sentinel detects deadlock (no heartbeat for 5s), starve watchdog to trigger systemd restart
- Codex: Keep sentinel active during Phases 1-5, disarm at Phase 6. If driver panics during Phase 6, catch_unwind to continue shutting down remaining drivers.
title: Implement Safety Sentinel RAII guard for daemon lifecycle

close_reason: Implemented, tested (58/58 pass), reviewed by 3-model panel (G3 Pro, Codex, Opus). Review fixes applied.
description: registry.shutdown_all() iterates drivers sequentially with no timeout. If a serial port driver hangs on close (common), daemon hangs forever, preventing systemd restart. Wrap in tokio::time::timeout(Duration::from_secs(5)). On timeout, log error, force-close, and continue exit. All three models flagged this as critical for production reliability.
title: Add shutdown timeout for hardware drivers

close_reason: Implemented, tested (58/58 pass), reviewed by 3-model panel (G3 Pro, Codex, Opus). Review fixes applied.
description:
CONFIRMED BUG (all 3 models): In main.rs:405-420, registry.shutdown_all() runs BEFORE HDF5 writer flush. Correct 6-phase shutdown sequence (from Codex DaemonManager design):
Phase 1 - Gate (1s timeout): Set shutting_down flag, reject new gRPC requests
Phase 2 - Abort (2s): Stop running scripts at next on_progress check
Phase 3 - StopAcquisition (2s): Stop camera/DAQ frame production
Phase 4 - Flush (5s): Drain ring buffer, flush HDF5 writer
Phase 5 - Safe (2s): Close laser shutters specifically
Phase 6 - Disconnect (5s): registry.shutdown_all() for all remaining hardware
Safety Sentinel disarmed only after Phase 6 completes.
NOTE: Must stop acquisition BEFORE drain, otherwise buffer keeps filling and never drains.
title: Fix shutdown ordering: flush storage before hardware shutdown

close_reason: Closed
description: Create integration test that asserts shutdown order: stop gRPC → flush storage → shutdown hardware. Use mock registry + mock writer with atomic ordering counters. Test must fail if order is reversed. Must be written BEFORE refactoring start_daemon() to serve as regression guard. GPT 5.2 Codex recommended creating this as first step of daemon lifecycle refactor.
title: Add shutdown ordering contract test

close_reason: Fixed: Upgraded egui ecosystem to 0.33 for Rerun 0.27.3 compatibility. Verified on maitai: daemon running with 7 hardware devices (Prime BSI camera, 3 ELL14 rotators, ESP300 stage, power meter, MaiTai laser), daq-rerun GUI connected and streaming via Rerun gRPC proxy on port 9876. Commit e370aa3c pushed to main.
description: P0 Blocker: Daemon crashes silently after ~35 frames when streaming from Prime BSI camera. No error in logs, no backtrace, no core dump. Crash happens during GUI frame streaming (h2 protocol error precedes crash). Serial hardware (ELL14, ESP300, MaiTai, Newport) works fine without camera.
design:
## Root Cause Analysis (Final)

**Confirmed: gRPC is fundamentally unsuitable for 240MB/s video streaming**

The h2 protocol errors and silent crashes are caused by HTTP/2 flow control exhaustion:
- 8MB messages at 30fps saturates default HTTP/2 windows
- Triple-copy pipeline: Camera → Vec<u8> → Protobuf → Network buffer
- Unbounded send buffers cause mimalloc/OOM issues
- Background thread panics don't propagate visibly

## Recommended Architecture: 'Mullet Strategy'

**Control Plane (gRPC)**: Keep for low-bandwidth commands (move, exposure, start/stop)
**Data Plane (Rerun)**: Use for high-bandwidth frame streaming (Arrow transport)

| Approach | Pros | Cons | Verdict |
|----------|------|------|---------|
| Fix gRPC | Minimal change | Fighting the tool | NOT RECOMMENDED |
| Rerun SDK | Robust, handles backpressure | Need Rerun viewer | RECOMMENDED |
| Shared Memory | Zero-copy | Local only | Use for disk IO |

## Implementation Plan

1. Disable custom `StreamFrames` gRPC endpoint
2. Log frames to `rerun::RecordingStream` in acquisition loop
3. Connect GUI to Rerun port (9876) for visualization
4. Keep gRPC port (50051) for commands only

Reference: `docs/architecture/rerun-refactoring-guide.md`

description:
## Context
An external review provided a development guide with significant inaccuracies about the rust-daq architecture. This epic documents the CORRECT state and provides authoritative clarification.

## External Review Errors Identified

### Error 1: "Split-Brain Transitional State"
**Claimed**: Repository is in transitional state between legacy and V5
**CORRECT**: V5 architecture is COMPLETE. Legacy V1-V4 code was removed (~295KB deleted). See CHANGELOG.md and git tag `phase-3-bd-232k-dead-code-removal`.

### Error 2: Legacy Driver Paths Still Exist
**Claimed**: Drivers at `crates/rust-daq/src/hardware/drivers/` need migration
**CORRECT**: Migration COMPLETE. Drivers are in `crates/daq-hardware/src/drivers/` (ell14.rs, esp300.rs, maitai.rs, mock.rs, newport_1830c.rs)

### Error 3: Repository Name
**Claimed**: Repository is "rudaq"
**CORRECT**: Private repo is "rust-daq" (TheFermiSea/rust-daq). Public mirror renamed to "rudaq" (easternanemone/rudaq).

### Error 4: GUI Status
**Claimed**: Deprecate daq-egui in favor of gui-tauri
**CORRECT**: daq-egui is the ACTIVE GUI. gui-tauri exists but is not the primary interface.

### Error 5: Introspectable Trait
**Claimed**: Every device must implement Introspectable for JSON schema
**CORRECT**: Capability traits are in daq-core/src/capabilities.rs. The primary traits are Movable, Readable, Triggerable, FrameProducer, ExposureControl. Introspectable is not the central pattern - Parameter<T> reactive system handles observability.

## Correct Architecture Summary

### Crate Structure (Verified)
- daq-core: Foundation types, Parameter<T>, capability traits
- daq-hardware: HAL + drivers (ELL14, ESP300, MaiTai, Newport 1830-C, Mock)
- daq-experiment: RunEngine and Plans
- daq-scripting: Rhai integration (optional, feature-gated)
- daq-server: gRPC server (optional, feature-gated)
- daq-storage: Ring buffer, Arrow, HDF5, CSV backends
- daq-proto: Protobuf definitions
- daq-egui: Active GUI
- daq-bin: CLI binaries
- rust-daq: Integration layer with prelude

### Key Patterns (Correct)
1. Parameter<T> reactive system (not Introspectable JSON schema)
2. Capability traits: Movable, Readable, Triggerable, FrameProducer
3. Feature-gated optional dependencies (server, scripting)
4. Headless-first architecture (daemon + clients)

## Action Items
Child tasks will address documentation updates based on these corrections.
title: Corrected Architecture Analysis: V5 State Assessment

description:
## Problem
Gemini 3 Pro code review found critical issue: get_or_create_runtime() is called INSIDE device iteration loops in shutter_safety.rs (lines 197, 274, 354).

If Handle::try_current() fails, it creates and leaks N runtimes (one per device), potentially causing OOM during panic cleanup.

## Fix
Move runtime creation OUTSIDE the loops:
1. Call get_or_create_runtime() once before the loop
2. Use the single handle reference inside the loop
3. Apply to emergency_close_all, emergency_stop_motors, emergency_zero_outputs

## Files
- crates/scripting/src/shutter_safety.rs

## References
- Gemini 3 Pro review (continuation_id: ef0136c4-c943-4cb8-b8e6-82e63725f5f2)
- Original epic: bd-d9nw
title: Fix runtime leak in panic hook emergency methods

description:
driver-comedi has pre-existing clippy warnings that fail CI with -D warnings:

## Issues

1. **missing_fields_in_debug** (3 occurrences)
   - `hal/analog_input.rs:139` - ReadableAnalogInput Debug impl missing `inner`, `range` fields
   - `hal/analog_output.rs:201` - SettableAnalogOutput Debug impl missing `range` field
   - `hal/digital_io.rs:266` - SwitchableDigitalIO Debug impl missing `directions` field

2. **ptr_cast_constness**
   - `multi_channel.rs:106` - Use `.cast_mut()` instead of `as *mut`

3. **ptr_as_ptr**
   - `streaming.rs:563` - Use `.cast::<c_void>()` instead of `as *mut c_void`

## Fix Options

### For Debug impls (preferred: finish_non_exhaustive)
```rust
impl Debug for ReadableAnalogInput {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        f.debug_struct("ReadableAnalogInput")
            .field("channel", &self.channel)
            // ... other fields
            .finish_non_exhaustive() // <-- instead of .finish()
    }
}
```

### For pointer casts
```rust
// Before
self.data.as_ptr().add(idx) as *mut f64

// After
self.data.as_ptr().add(idx).cast_mut()
```

## Context
Discovered during bd-995i PR CI run. These issues exist on main.
title: Fix driver-comedi clippy warnings

design:
# v0.8.0: Advanced Features - Competitive with Commercial Platforms

## Goal
Transform rust-daq into production-ready platform competitive with LabView/Bluesky.

## Key Features
1. SQL run history database (searchable metadata)
2. Jupyter kernel integration (Python control via gRPC)
3. Adaptive experiment engine (Bayesian optimization, active learning)
4. Advanced visualizations (3D plots, heatmaps, waterfall)
5. Driver marketplace (community contributions)
6. Authentication/TLS for server (security)

## Target Users
- Non-programmer laboratory researchers
- Multi-user lab environments
- Production deployments

## Dependencies
- Blocked by v0.7.0 completion (bd-94zq)

## Complexity: XL

close_reason: Fixed: replaced unwrap() with idiomatic tuple pattern matching. Function now validates exactly one hex digit without any panic-prone code. All 200 daq-hardware tests pass including 7 ELL14-specific tests.
description:
## Location
`crates/daq-hardware/src/registry.rs:248`

## Problem
The `validate_ell14_address()` function uses `.unwrap()` after a length check:

```rust
let addr_char = address.chars().next().unwrap();  // Line 248
```

This violates the project's guideline to eliminate panic-prone unwrap/expect in production code.

## Evidence
- Commit bca0b5f4: fix(egui): eliminate panic-prone unwrap/expect
- Commit 04fc6da2: fix(egui): eliminate production unwrap()/expect() calls

## Fix
Replace with defensive error handling:
```rust
let addr_char = address.chars().next()
    .ok_or_else(|| DaqError::Configuration(
        "Invalid ELL14 address: empty string".to_string()
    ))?;
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- No unwrap() in validate_ell14_address
- Unit tests pass
- Clippy clean
title: Fix unwrap() in validate_ell14_address

close_reason: Completed end-to-end Mechelle 5000 + Andor iSTAR echelleogram-to-spectrum delivery in rust-daq across all workstreams: profile schema/validation, extraction preview, spectrum visualization/streaming, real HG-2 datasets and regression harnesses, sidecar prototype, calibration GUI MVP workflows, docs, and rollout guidance.
description:
Goal: deliver end-to-end echelleogram-to-spectrum support for the Andor iSTAR sCMOS + Mechelle 5000 workflow in rust-daq, beginning with an MVP extraction path and evolving toward robust calibration UX and native/streamed spectrum visualization.

Scope:
- Convert 2D echelle frames into usable 1D spectra in rust-daq.
- Support calibration artifacts (order traces, wavelength solution, blaze/flat, masks) as reusable profiles.
- Integrate with existing image viewer and/or live visualization UI paths.
- Evaluate external reduction reuse (especially GAMSE) via a Python sidecar and define long-term architecture.

Constraints and current findings:
- rust-daq already has Measurement::Spectrum with x-axis + amplitudes + metadata support.
- ImageViewerPanel has direct access to raw frame bytes at process_frame(), making local MVP extraction feasible.
- Current gRPC measurement stream scalarizes spectra and cannot deliver full arrays to UI as-is.
- Shamrock pixel calibration support exists, but Mechelle 5000 requires full echelle tracing/extraction/wavelength calibration workflow.

Non-goals for initial MVP:
- Full optimal extraction parity with mature astronomy pipelines.
- Automated line identification and fully interactive calibration UI on day one.
- Immediate protocol-wide spectrum streaming redesign before proving extraction path.

Success criteria (epic-level):
1. rust-daq can show a derived spectrum from live or recorded camera frames using a calibration profile.
2. Calibration artifacts can be persisted/reloaded with schema validation and provenance metadata.
3. Numerical outputs can be compared against reference reductions with defined tolerances.
4. A documented path exists for Phase 2 sidecar integration and Phase 3 protocol-native spectrum streaming.
title: Mechelle 5000 echelleogram-to-spectrum pipeline in rust-daq

**Date:** 2026-01-28
**Machine:** maitai-eos
**Result:** 1495 PASS, 11 unique FAIL (138 total with retries)

## Critical Test Failures

### 1. Comedi Feature Flag Issue (SIGABRT)

- **Root Cause:** Test runs without comedi-sdk but calls comedi functions

### 2. Colorbar Midpoint Tests (2 tests, 4 failures with binary)

### 3. E2E Script Execution (3 tests)
- `test_e2e_script_count_plan`
- `test_e2e_script_linescan_plan`
- `test_e2e_script_multiple_plans`
- **Root Cause:** Script execution framework issues

### 4. Frame Observer Timing
- `test_channel_based_observer_pattern`
- **Root Cause:** Timing-sensitive test

### 5. ELL14 Retry Logic
- `test_get_device_info_retries_on_truncated_response`
- **Root Cause:** Retry logic not working as expected

### 6. gRPC Frame Rate Metrics

close_reason: Audit complete — all std::thread::sleep uses are in spawn_blocking contexts or intentionally blocking code.
description:
## Pattern Identified (Gemini Validation)

Two timing-related test failures (bd-2m11.4, bd-2m11.6) suggest a potential pattern.

### Problem:
`std::thread::sleep` in async functions blocks the entire tokio executor thread, causing:
- Timing test flakiness
- Unpredictable frame rates
- Race conditions

### Action:
```bash
rg 'std::thread::sleep' --type rust crates/
```

### Fix Pattern:
Replace:
```rust
std::thread::sleep(Duration::from_millis(100));
```

With:
```rust
tokio::time::sleep(Duration::from_millis(100)).await;
```

### Note:
This could fix MULTIPLE timing tests, not just the two identified.
title: Audit for std::thread::sleep in async code

description: Create a tool to repair/recover data from corrupt HDF5 files caused by power loss or daemon crash mid-write. Tool should: (1) detect and report corruption, (2) recover valid data up to the last successful flush, (3) optionally rebuild the file structure. Identified as missing by Gemini g3-pro analysis (2026-02-08).

description:
Second phase of GUI improvements focusing on advanced widgets and features tailored for experimental physicists, microscopists, and spectroscopists.

## Background

Following the successful Phase 1 visual overhaul (bd-pgb2), this epic adds functional enhancements inspired by thz-image-explorer and common needs in scientific instrumentation:

- Advanced selection widgets (double slider, polygon ROI)
- Enhanced visualization controls (interactive colorbar)
- Better organization (settings window)
- Scientific analysis tools (crosshair cursor, measurement tools, metadata overlay)

## Target Users
- Experimental physicists
- Microscopists (optical, electron, scanning probe)
- Spectroscopists (Raman, FTIR, fluorescence)
- Anyone doing quantitative image analysis

## Scope
- ✅ IN SCOPE: New widgets, visualization enhancements, scientific tools
- ❌ OUT OF SCOPE: Core architecture changes, new hardware support
notes:
## Detailed Integration Strategy & Recommended Crates

1. **Range Selection (Double Slider)** [Priority: High]
   - **Crate**: `egui_double_slider`
   - **Plan**: Replace paired min/max sliders in Histogram/FFT panels. Create wrapper in `widgets/double_slider.rs`.

2. **Modal Dialogs (Settings)** [Priority: High]
   - **Crate**: `egui_modal` + `egui-file-dialog`
   - **Plan**: Create `SettingsDialog` struct triggered via menu bar.

3. **Keyboard Shortcuts** [Priority: High]
   - **Crate**: `egui_hotkey`
   - **Plan**: Central `ShortcutManager` in `app.rs`.

4. **Scripting/REPL**
   - **Crate**: `egui_code_editor` (syntax highlighting) + `egui_logger`.
   - **Ref**: `serial-monitor-rust` command history pattern.

5. **Instrument Diagram**
   - **Crate**: `egui_cable` (v0.9).
   - **Plan**: New panel for visualizing trigger/data routing.

6. **Preferences**
   - **Crate**: `preferences`
   - **Plan**: Migrate theme and window state persistence.
title: GUI Enhancement Phase 2: Advanced Widgets and Scientific Visualization

description:
Worktree: feat/config-foundation (branch from main)

**Phase 1: Config Foundation**
1.1 Fix daemon address split-brain — Remove standalone save/load in UI connection module, all persistence flows through AppSettings.connection.daemon_address. Add AddressSource::Preset variant. Migrate orphaned "daemon_address" eframe key on first load.
1.2 Add config validation to ServerConfig — validate() method checking port range (1024-65535), ring buffer path parent exists, output_directory writable. Called after load().
1.3 Extract hardcoded paths — bin/main.rs ring buffer path from ServerConfig, require --hardware-config with --lab-hardware.

**Phase 2: GUI Config + Presets**
2.1 Create config/gui.toml — checked-in presets (Maitai Lab, Local Mock, Maitai Direct) + GUI defaults (theme, font_scale, auto_reconnect).
2.2 Create crates/ui/src/gui_config.rs — types (GuiConfig, GuiDefaults, DaemonPreset, SshConfig), config discovery (CWD → exe_dir → ~/.config/rust-daq), loader, merge, SSH validation.
2.3 Wire into DaqApp constructor — settings load order: default → gui.toml → eframe persisted → CLI overrides. Add gui_presets field.
2.4 Render presets in Daemon menu — after Lab Hardware button, separator + Presets heading, buttons with tooltips, default marked with star.
2.5 --preset CLI flag — loads gui.toml, finds preset by name, creates DaemonMode.
2.6 Register module — mod gui_config in UI main.rs.

**Files modified**:
- crates/ui/src/connection.rs — remove standalone save/load
- crates/client/src/connection.rs — AddressSource::Preset, migration helper
- crates/ui/src/app.rs — remove duplicate persistence, gui_presets field, constructor merge, Daemon menu presets
- crates/server/src/config.rs — validate() method
- crates/bin/src/main.rs — config for ring buffer path, --hardware-config requirement

close_reason:
FIXED: Implemented sequence mode streaming as workaround for circular buffer stall.

Root Cause Analysis:
- CIRC_OVERWRITE fails with Error 185 on Prime BSI (invalid config with callbacks)
- CIRC_NO_OVERWRITE stalls after ~85 frames (~1.7 seconds)

Solution:
- Implemented sequence mode streaming (pl_exp_setup_seq + pl_exp_start_seq)
- Acquires batches of 20 frames, restarts for continuous streaming
- Tests 15-17 validated sequence mode works on Prime BSI

Results:
- 10-second test: 281 frames @ 27.1 FPS, 0 errors
- Compare to circular buffer: stalled at ~85 frames
- Issue FULLY RESOLVED

Commits:
- 6e7217af: test(pvcam): add Tests 16-17 for sequence mode variations
- cb2628ae: feat(pvcam): implement sequence mode streaming (bd-3gnv)
- 89497d60: fix(pvcam): use Frame::from_u16 in sequence mode loop
description: During long-running streaming tests (10s), frame delivery stalls after approximately 1 second. Initial frames arrive at expected FPS but then stop arriving entirely. Smoke tests (1s duration) work fine. Observed on Prime BSI camera at multiple exposure times (10ms, 33ms, 100ms). May be related to circular buffer handling, broadcast channel capacity, or frame delivery loop.
title: PVCAM streaming stall after ~1 second

close_reason: Implemented in feat/streaming-performance-improvements branch
description:
**Location:** crates/daq-server/src/grpc/hardware_service.rs (~line 1160)

**Issue:** The streaming loop uses `if (current_value - *last_value).abs() > f64::EPSILON` for change detection. If a sensor jitters by 1e-10 (noise), it floods the stream with updates.

**Fix:** Use a configurable deadband/threshold for change detection instead of EPSILON. Allow clients to specify minimum change threshold in the request, defaulting to a sensible value (e.g., 0.001).

**Impact:** Potential stream flooding from sensor noise
title: MEDIUM: Add configurable deadband for StreamObservables

close_reason: Audit complete. Documented 15 used SDK functions, 3 implemented traits, identified gaps for all 5 remaining Sub-Epic 5 tasks, and found architecture observations (stale state, precision loss, missing presence checks).
description: Verify ShamrockSetGrating, SetWavelength, SetAutoSlitWidth, SetFlipperMirror, SetShutter match expected SDK calling conventions. Key file: crates/driver-andor-sdk3/src/spectrograph.rs (694 lines)
notes:
## Audit: spectrograph.rs vs Shamrock SDK

### Currently Implemented (15 SDK functions used)
- Library lifecycle: ShamrockInitialize, ShamrockClose
- Device enumeration: GetNumberDevices, GetSerialNumber
- Grating: SetGrating, GetNumberGratings, GetGratingInfo
- Wavelength: SetWavelength, GetWavelength
- Slit: SetAutoSlitWidth, GetAutoSlitWidth
- Calibration: GetCalibration
- Shutter: SetShutter
- Flipper: SetFlipperMirror

### Capability Traits Implemented
- WavelengthTunable ✓ (set/get wavelength, wavelength_range)
- ShutterControl ✓ (open/close shutter, is_shutter_open)
- Parameterized ✓ (parameter registry)
- SpectrometerControl — EXISTS in capabilities.rs but NOT implemented

### Gaps by Sub-Epic 5 Task
1. bd-8n55 (filter wheel): Missing FilterIsPresent/Get/Set/GetFilterInfo + parameter
2. bd-2in1 (focus mirror): Missing FocusMirrorIsPresent/Get/Set/GetMaxSteps + parameter
3. bd-1rfx (detector offset): Missing GetDetectorOffset/SetDetectorOffset + parameter
4. bd-p1zz (wavelength limits): Missing GetWavelengthLimits; wavelength_range() hardcoded to (200,1000)
5. bd-2qd1 (multi-port slit): Only port 2 hardcoded; no per-port parameters; missing AutoSlitIsPresent/SlitIsPresent/AutoSlitReset

### Other Observations
- get_grating() returns cached Parameter value, not SDK readback (stale if changed externally)
- is_shutter_open() uses AtomicBool, not ShamrockGetShutter (state can drift)
- ShamrockSetWavelength takes f32 but Parameter<f64> — precision loss
- No feature presence checking (GratingIsPresent, FlipperMirrorIsPresent, etc.)
- No optical params query (EepromGetOpticalParams)
- No pixel width/count config before calibration (SetPixelWidth, SetNumberPixels)
- No grating offset support (Get/SetGratingOffset)
title: 5.1: Audit spectrograph implementation against SDK docs

description:
Integrate LIBS (Laser-Induced Breakdown Spectroscopy) hardware and experiments from the Python LIBS codebase into the rust-daq unified framework.

## Overview
Port the LIBS Python codebase to rust-daq, enabling unified instrument control for both RASHG (maitai) and LIBS systems.

## Hardware Inventory (NEW - different from RASHG)
1. **Andor iStar sCMOS Camera** - Gated ICCD with DDG, MCP gain (Windows SDK)
2. **Andor ATSpectrograph** - Grating control, wavelength calibration (Windows SDK)
3. **Dover Motion XYZ Stages** - High-speed 3-axis, Trigger-On-Position (C++ API, Linux support!)
4. **Spirit Laser** - Picosecond laser via CANopen protocol
5. **NI-DAQmx** - Triggering/pulse generation (Windows)

## Key Experiments to Port
- Single-shot/multi-shot spectroscopy
- Step scans (X, Y, Z, 2D grids)
- Trigger-On-Position rapid acquisition
- Delay scans (DDG timing)
- Radiance calibration

## Architecture Notes
- Dover Motion has C++ API with Linux support (no PyO3 needed!)
- Andor SDK3 requires FFI bindings via bindgen
- Spirit laser can use existing Rust CANopen crates
- Feature flag: `libs` for LIBS-specific drivers

## Documentation
- Dover Motion API: LIBS/docs/dover-motion-api/
- Original Python code: LIBS/initialization.py, LIBS/experiments.py
notes:
Reparented 2026-02-10 into phased backlog hierarchy (Phase 4: LIBS Integration Delivery).
\n2026-02-19 triage: tracking epic; execute child tasks directly.
title: EPIC: LIBS Integration into rust-daq

close_reason: Windows CI workflow added (.github/workflows/libs-windows.yml): build verification, mock-mode tests, clippy checks on windows-latest runner
description:
Set up GitHub Actions CI for Windows builds and tests.

## Scope
- Windows build verification
- Mock-mode tests on Windows
- Hardware tests (manual trigger when hardware available)

## CI Configuration
```yaml
# .github/workflows/windows-libs.yml
name: LIBS Windows Build

on:
  push:
    paths:
      - 'crates/driver-dover-motion/**'
      - 'crates/driver-andor-sdk3/**'
      - 'crates/driver-spirit-laser/**'
      - 'crates/driver-nidaqmx/**'
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'crates/driver-dover-motion/**'
      - 'crates/driver-andor-sdk3/**'

jobs:
  build-windows:
    runs-on: windows-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Install Rust
        uses: dtolnay/rust-action@stable
        with:
          targets: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc
      
      - name: Build LIBS drivers (mock mode)
        run: cargo build --features libs -p bin
      
      - name: Run mock tests
        run: cargo test --features libs
      
  # Manual hardware tests (self-hosted runner on LIBS machine)
  hardware-tests:
    runs-on: [self-hosted, windows, libs-machine]
    if: github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Run hardware smoke tests
        run: cargo test --features libs_hardware --test libs_smoke_tests
        env:
          LIBS_HARDWARE_TEST: 1
```

## Self-Hosted Runner Setup
Document setup for LIBS Windows machine as GitHub Actions runner:
1. Install GitHub Actions runner
2. Configure as 'libs-machine' label
3. Ensure SDK paths in environment

## Deliverables
- .github/workflows/windows-libs.yml
- Self-hosted runner setup documentation
- Badge for Windows build status
title: Phase 4.2: Windows CI/CD pipeline

description:
Create low-level FFI bindings for Andor SDK3 (camera + spectrograph).

## Scope
- Use bindgen to generate Rust bindings from atcore.h
- Target: Camera control, DDG timing, MCP gain, gating modes
- Windows-only (SDK3 is Windows-only)

## Key Functions to Bind
### Camera (atcore.dll)
- AT_InitialiseLibrary(), AT_FinaliseLibrary()
- AT_Open(), AT_Close()
- AT_SetInt(), AT_SetFloat(), AT_SetEnumString()
- AT_GetInt(), AT_GetFloat(), AT_GetEnumString()
- AT_Command() - for AcquisitionStart/Stop
- AT_QueueBuffer(), AT_WaitBuffer()

### Spectrograph (atspectrograph.dll)
- ATSpectrographInitialise(), ATSpectrographClose()
- ATSpectrographSetGrating(), ATSpectrographGetGrating()
- ATSpectrographSetWavelength(), ATSpectrographGetWavelength()
- ATSpectrographSetSlitWidth()
- ATSpectrographGetCalibration() - returns wavelength array
- ATSpectrographSetShutter()

## Challenge: Wide Strings
Andor SDK uses Windows WCHAR (UTF-16) for enum string values. Need careful conversion:
```rust
// Convert Rust str -> Windows wide string
let wide: Vec<u16> = mode.encode_utf16().chain(Some(0)).collect();
```

- `andor-sdk3-sys` crate
- build.rs linking to atcore.lib, atspectrograph.lib

description:
Create safe Rust wrapper for Andor iStar camera and spectrograph.

## Scope
### Camera Driver
- Implement FrameProducer for frame streaming
- Implement GatedCamera trait (NEW) for ICCD-specific features
- Handle DDG timing, MCP gain, gating modes

### Spectrograph Driver
- Implement SpectrometerControl trait (NEW)
- Grating selection, wavelength control, slit width
- Wavelength calibration array retrieval

## New Capability Traits
```rust
#[async_trait]
pub trait GatedCamera: FrameProducer {
    async fn set_gate_mode(&mut self, mode: GateMode) -> Result<()>;
    async fn set_ddg_timing(&mut self, delay_ps: u64, width_ps: u64) -> Result<()>;
    async fn set_mcp_gain(&mut self, gain: u16) -> Result<()>;
    async fn set_intelligate(&mut self, enabled: bool) -> Result<()>;
}

pub enum GateMode { CwOn, Ddg, FireAndForget }

#[async_trait]
pub trait SpectrometerControl {
    async fn set_grating(&mut self, grating_num: u8) -> Result<()>;
    async fn set_wavelength(&mut self, nm: f64) -> Result<()>;
    async fn set_slit_width(&mut self, slit: u8, width_um: u16) -> Result<()>;

    async fn set_shutter(&mut self, open: bool) -> Result<()>;
}
```

description:
Create driver for Spirit picosecond laser using CANopen protocol.

## Scope
- Implement Spirit laser control via CANopen/IXXAT
- Use existing Rust `canopen` crate for protocol
- Implement existing traits: ShutterControl, EmissionControl

## CANopen Commands (from spirit_laser_simple.py)
- Control (0x2000:00): 0=Off, 1=Standby, 2=On
- Shutter (0x2504:00): 0=Close, 1=Open
- Node ID: 0x0E (14)

## Available Rust Crates
- `canopen` - CANopen protocol stack
- `socketcan` (Linux) or IXXAT SDK (Windows)
- `tokio-socketcan` for async

## Implementation
```rust
pub struct SpiritLaserDriver {
    master: CANopenMaster,
    node_id: u8,  // 0x0E
}

#[async_trait]
impl EmissionControl for SpiritLaserDriver {
    async fn enable_emission(&mut self) -> Result<()> {
        // SDO write 2 to 0x2000:00
        self.master.sdo_write_u8(self.node_id, 0x2000, 0x00, 2).await
    }
    async fn disable_emission(&mut self) -> Result<()> {
        self.master.sdo_write_u8(self.node_id, 0x2000, 0x00, 0).await
    }
}

#[async_trait]
impl ShutterControl for SpiritLaserDriver {
    async fn open_shutter(&mut self) -> Result<()> {
        self.master.sdo_write_u8(self.node_id, 0x2504, 0x00, 1).await
    }
    async fn close_shutter(&mut self) -> Result<()> {
        self.master.sdo_write_u8(self.node_id, 0x2504, 0x00, 0).await
    }
}
```

## Configuration
```toml
[devices.spirit_laser]
type = "spirit_laser"
interface = "can0"  # Linux socketcan
node_id = 14
```

## Platform Notes
- Linux: Use socketcan kernel interface
- Windows: Requires IXXAT USB adapter SDK bindings

## Deliverables
- `driver-spirit-laser` crate
- SpiritLaserFactory
- Mock driver for testing
title: Phase 1.5: Create driver-spirit-laser crate

# Pulse generation for camera triggering
task.co_channels.add_co_pulse_chan_time(
    "Dev1/ctr0",

    low_time=0.001
)
task.timing.cfg_implicit_timing(

pub struct NiDaqTrigger {

- Store dimension_names in ARROW:extension:metadata JSON payload

description:
Add uom crate to daq-core for compile-time unit checking - NARROWED SCOPE TO DRIVER INTERNALS ONLY.

**Decision (Gemini review):** uom only inside drivers for hardware safety. f64 at public API boundaries.

**Rationale:** 
- Mars Climate Orbiter factor - physical safety is non-negotiable for hardware control
- But uom generics infect call stack if used everywhere
- Solution: Encapsulate to driver internals (the 'danger zone')

**Types to create:**
- `PowerReading` with `uom::si::f64::Power` (internal)
- `wavelength_nm()` helper returning `Length` (internal)
- Driver internals use typed values
- **External trait APIs remain f64 for compatibility**

**Example pattern:**
```rust
// Internal (uom)
fn set_wavelength_internal(&self, wl: Length) { ... }

// Public API (f64)
async fn set_wavelength(&self, nm: f64) -> Result<()> {
    let wl = Length::new::<nanometer>(nm);
    self.set_wavelength_internal(wl)
}
```

**Files to modify/create:**
- crates/daq-core/Cargo.toml (add uom = "0.36")
- crates/daq-core/src/units.rs (NEW - internal unit helpers)
- Driver crates: use uom internally, convert at API boundary

**Owner:** core-supervisor
notes: Deferred - orchestrator investigation found no unit conversion bugs in drivers. All tests pass with current f64 approach using correct .round() conversions. Complexity of uom integration + 30 transitive deps not justified. Revisit only if production bugs emerge.

description:
# Technical Debt Epic: Driver & Scripting Infrastructure

Comprehensive technical debt cleanup identified by Gemini and Codex code review.

## High Priority Issues

### H1: GenericSerialDriver Silent Timeout Failures
- **Location**: `generic_serial.rs:600-669`, `generic_serial.rs:827-907`
- **Issue**: `transaction` and `transaction_with_timeout` return `Ok("")` on timeout instead of error
- **Impact**: Masks hardware disconnects, causes downstream parsing errors
- **Fix**: Return timeout error when response buffer is empty

### H2: MaiTai Wavelength Tuning Race Condition
- **Location**: `maitai.rs` - `set_wavelength` / `attach_wavelength_callbacks`
- **Issue**: Returns after 50ms without verifying laser reached target wavelength
- **Impact**: Scripts execute data acquisition while laser still tuning, corrupting data
- **Fix**: Implement `wait_settled` polling `read:wav?` until target reached

### H3: DeviceComponents/Capabilities Mismatch
- **Location**: `factory.rs:656-667`, `factory.rs:520-536`
- **Issue**: Builds DeviceComponents with all traits unconditionally regardless of config
- **Impact**: Registry advertises unsupported capabilities, runtime errors on invocation
- **Fix**: Construct DeviceComponents based on `device.capabilities` or `trait_mapping.*`

## Medium Priority Issues

### M1: Serial Settings Ignored in Factory
- **Location**: `factory.rs:618-624`
- **Issue**: Hardcodes 8N1/no flow control, ignores config values like `data_bits`, `parity`
- **Impact**: Devices requiring non-default serial settings won't work
- **Fix**: Map `DeviceConfig.connection` into tokio_serial builder

### M2: Shutter Registry Lifecycle Gaps
- **Location**: `bindings.rs:743-758`
- **Issue**: Registers in ShutterRegistry before opening; doesn't unregister on failure
- **Impact**: Stale registry entries, emergency close attempts on never-opened shutters
- **Fix**: Register after successful open or use scope guard

### M3: HeartbeatShutterGuard State Inconsistency
- **Location**: `shutter_safety.rs:381-419`, `shutter_safety.rs:470-479`
- **Issue**: Watchdog closes shutter on timeout but doesn't update `is_open`; `close()` doesn't stop watchdog
- **Impact**: `is_open()` returns stale state, redundant close attempts
- **Fix**: Set `is_open` false on timeout, have `close()` abort watchdog

### M4: Non-Numeric Config Parameters Ignored
- **Location**: `generic_serial.rs:201-206`
- **Issue**: Only loads numeric parameter defaults (`as_f64()`), ignores strings/booleans
- **Impact**: Config values appear supported but are dropped
- **Fix**: Store parameters as richer type or validate/reject unsupported types

### M5: Memory Leak in Factory Introspection
- **Location**: `factory.rs:556-569`
- **Issue**: `Box::leak` on every `driver_type()`, `name()`, `capabilities()` call
- **Impact**: Unbounded heap growth if introspection called frequently
- **Fix**: Leak once in constructor, use `OnceLock`/`Lazy` to cache

### M6: Newport 1830-C Performance Bottleneck
- **Location**: `newport_1830c.rs` - `query_power`
- **Issue**: Sends `U1` command before every power reading
- **Impact**: Doubles serial traffic, adds ~100ms latency per reading
- **Fix**: Check/set units periodically or on error only

### M7: ELL14 Fragile Bus Logic
- **Location**: `ell14.rs` - `transaction_once`
- **Issue**: "Read until 3x silence" loop adds ~15ms latency to every command
- **Impact**: Limited command throughput, non-deterministic behavior
- **Fix**: Replace with strict frame parsing or extract to shared Rs485Bus utility

## Low Priority Issues

### L1: Blocking Sleep in Scripting
- **Location**: `bindings.rs:662-666`
- **Issue**: Uses `std::thread::sleep` in `read_averaged`
- **Impact**: Blocks Tokio worker thread, reduces concurrency
- **Fix**: Use `tokio::time::sleep` in async helper

### L2: Duplicate DriverFactory Concepts
- **Location**: `factory.rs:140-348` vs `daq-core/src/driver.rs:473-520`
- **Issue**: Two "DriverFactory" concepts with duplicated protocol mapping
- **Impact**: Higher maintenance cost
- **Fix**: Rename hardware struct or factor shared mapping into single helper

### L3: Binary Commands Not Implemented
- **Location**: `config/devices/modbus_example.toml:66-140`
- **Issue**: Defines `binary_commands` but GenericSerialDriver has no implementation
- **Impact**: Misleading config
- **Fix**: Mark as unsupported in docs or gate under feature

### L4: Flaky Timing Tests
- **Location**: `shutter_safety.rs:580+`
- **Issue**: Tests use real sleeps instead of paused time
- **Impact**: Potential CI flakiness
- **Fix**: Convert to `#[tokio::test(start_paused = true)]`

## Refactoring Opportunities

### R1: Extract Shared Serial Buffer Draining Logic
- **Issue**: "Aggressive buffer draining" logic duplicated in 3/4 drivers
- **Fix**: Extract into shared `SerialUtils` trait or `Rs485Bus` utility

## Positives to Preserve
- Clear safety documentation in shutter_safety.rs
- Defense-in-depth layering in scripting bindings
- Guarded `run_blocking` to avoid runtime deadlocks
- Structured init sequence validation with configurable retries
- Correct signed integer handling in ELL14
- Proper `spawn_blocking` for serial port opens
notes:
## Codex Validation Round 2 (2026-01-23, continuation_id: 458e7928-2b76-4cd6-961d-6382420d7b68)

### H2, M6, M7 Now Validated

**H2: MaiTai Wavelength Race - VALIDATED as Medium (downgraded from High)**
Location: `crates/daq-driver-spectra-physics/src/maitai.rs:252-308` (attach_wavelength_callbacks) + `:576-577` (set_wavelength)
- Issue: `set_wavelength` returns after ~50ms parameter write without polling `read:wav?` to confirm target reached
- Impact: Scripts can take data at wrong wavelength during fast scans
- Fix options:
  1. Add `set_wavelength_and_wait(target, tol, timeout)` that polls `query_wavelength`
  2. Add optional 'wait_for_settle' flag in `set_wavelength` used by scripts
- Positives to preserve: Clear protocol comments and explicit logging around hardware interactions

**M6: Newport 1830-C U1 - VALIDATED as Low**
Location: `crates/daq-driver-newport/src/newport_1830c.rs:348-357` (query_power)
- Issue: Always sends `U1` before every `D?` read - adds ~100ms overhead per read
- Note: This is INTENTIONAL for robustness (handles front-panel unit changes)
- Impact: Throttles high-rate sampling
- Fix options:
  1. Make behavior configurable
  2. Reassert units only on cadence or when parsing indicates drift (`U?` on failure)
- Positives to preserve: Explicit unit-format commentary and parsing checks

**M7: ELL14 Transaction Latency - VALIDATED as Low**
Location: `crates/daq-driver-thorlabs/src/ell14.rs:817-934` (transaction_once)
- Issue: '3x silence' drain + short sleeps adds 10-20ms latency per command even on idle bus
- Impact: Reduces throughput for tight polling/scan loops
- Fix options:
  1. Make drain behavior configurable (off for point-to-point)
  2. Reduce zero-byte count/clear deadline
  3. Gate behind 'shared bus' flag
- Positives to preserve: Robust RS-485 handling (prefix scanning, response framing) is well-considered for noisy buses

### L4 Testing Strategy Resolution

**Recommendation**: Use `#[tokio::test(start_paused = true)]` - most idiomatic approach

Implementation:
- Attribute pauses time BEFORE test body runs, preventing accidental real sleeps
- Drive time forward with `tokio::time::advance(...)`
- Use `tokio::time::Instant` (NOT `std::time::Instant`) for assertions
- Avoids `tokio::time::pause()` inside test which can allow real time to elapse before pause

### Final Priority Summary After Validation

| ID | Issue | Final Priority | Origin |
|----|-------|---------------|--------|
| H1 | GenericSerialDriver timeout | HIGH | Codex |
| H3 | DeviceComponents mismatch | HIGH | Codex |
| H2 | MaiTai wavelength race | MEDIUM | Gemini (validated by Codex) |
| M1 | Serial settings ignored | MEDIUM/HIGH* | Codex |
| M2 | Shutter registry lifecycle | MEDIUM/HIGH* | Codex |
| M3 | HeartbeatShutterGuard state | MEDIUM/HIGH* | Codex |
| M4 | Non-numeric params ignored | MEDIUM | Codex |
| M5 | Memory leak Box::leak | MEDIUM | Codex |
| M6 | Newport U1 overhead | LOW | Gemini (validated by Codex) |
| M7 | ELL14 transaction latency | LOW | Gemini (validated by Codex) |
| L1 | Blocking sleep | LOW | Codex |
| L2 | Duplicate DriverFactory | LOW | Codex |
| L3 | Binary commands undocumented | LOW | Codex |
| L4 | Flaky timing tests | LOW | Codex |
| R1 | Extract serial drain logic | REFACTOR | Gemini |

*Elevate to HIGH if safety-critical shutters or non-8N1 devices in production
title: Technical Debt: Driver & Scripting Infrastructure

notes:
## Architecture Design Context

### LOAD BEFORE STARTING
- SKILL: rust-guidelines (FFI patterns, safety requirements, M-UNSAFE guidelines)
- SKILL: rust-async-patterns (channel patterns, threading models)
- READ: docs/architecture/analysis-pvcam-performance-gap.md (root cause)
- READ: temp_repro/stall_test.cpp (SDK reference implementation)

### Problem to Solve
Current design copies 8MB per frame because:
1. Broadcast channel requires Arc<Frame> with owned data
2. Frame must outlive SDK buffer slot (which gets reused)
3. Cannot send reference to SDK memory

### SDK Pattern (from LiveImage.cpp)
```cpp
// SDK allocates circular buffer in USER memory
uns8* circBuffer = new uns8[circBufferBytes];
pl_exp_start_cont(hcam, circBuffer, circBufferBytes);

// Callback gets pointer INTO circBuffer
void EofHandler(...) {
    pl_exp_get_latest_frame(hcam, &ctx->eofFrame);  // ptr into circBuffer
}

// Main loop reads directly from eofFrame pointer
// NO COPY - just reads from shared memory
```

### Constraint: Thread Safety
- Callback runs on PVCAM thread (not Rust async runtime)
- Frame loop runs on dedicated std::thread
- Broadcast subscribers run on tokio runtime
- GUI updates may lag behind frame production

### Design Options to Evaluate

1. **Pre-allocated Frame Pool**
   - Allocate N Frame buffers at stream start
   - Copy SDK data into pool slot (still copies, but no malloc)
   - Return to pool when Arc refcount drops
   - Pro: Eliminates malloc overhead
   - Con: Still copies data

2. **Ring Buffer with Subscriber Cursors**
   - Single large ring buffer owned by driver
   - Subscribers get read cursors, not owned copies
   - Pro: True zero-copy
   - Con: Complex lifetime management, backpressure handling

3. **Shared Memory with COW**
   - Map SDK buffer as shared memory
   - Use Arc<[u8]> with copy-on-write semantics
   - Pro: Zero-copy if no mutation
   - Con: Complex, may not work with SDK buffer lifecycle

4. **Hybrid: Pool + Lazy Copy**
   - Use pool for fast path
   - Only copy if subscriber needs to retain frame
   - Pro: Best of both worlds
   - Con: Complexity

### Requirements
- Must work with tokio::sync::broadcast (or replacement)
- Must handle 50+ FPS sustained
- Must not leak memory under backpressure
- Must maintain thread safety
- GUI streaming must work (may be slower consumer)

### Rust Guidelines to Apply (from rust-guidelines skill)
- M-UNSAFE: Document safety requirements for any unsafe blocks
- M-PUBLIC-DEBUG: All public types must impl Debug
- M-HOT-PATH: Optimize allocation in hot paths
- M-FFI: Proper FFI patterns for callback context

### Anti-Patterns to Avoid
- Holding SDK buffer locked while waiting on slow consumer
- Unbounded memory growth under backpressure
- Complex lifetime annotations that hurt maintainability
title: Phase 2: Design zero-copy frame delivery architecture

description:
## Decision

Gemini 3.1 analysis action items are organized as a standalone epic (bd-joh9) that is a sibling of the 5 phase epics under bd-9j1q, rather than being distributed into existing phases or nested within any single phase.

## Rationale

The 8 action items span multiple existing phases — safety (Phase 1/reliability), schema migrations (Phase 2/architecture), code quality (Phase 3/lint+safety), and long-horizon features (Phase 5/backlog). Distributing them across phases would fragment the provenance trail back to the Gemini analysis. A cross-cutting sibling epic preserves the analytical origin while allowing individual tasks to be worked in parallel with phase-gated work.

Additionally, bd-9j1q.1 through bd-9j1q.5 are already allocated to the five development phases. Creating bd-joh9 as an independent child avoids ID collision and keeps the phase numbering stable.

## Alternatives Considered

- **Distribute tasks into existing phase epics**: Rejected because items span 4 of 5 phases, losing the cohesive "Gemini review" provenance. Would make it harder to track which analysis recommendations have been addressed.
- **Create a top-level epic outside bd-9j1q**: Rejected because bd-9j1q is the single roadmap root per project convention ("all open epics should remain descendants of bd-9j1q"). A top-level epic would break the roadmap hierarchy.
- **Single flat list of tasks (no sub-epics)**: Rejected because the 8 items naturally cluster into 3 domains (scripting, experiment lifecycle, data plane) with internal dependencies. Sub-epics provide grouping and the schema v7 migration dependency is cleaner within the experiment sub-epic.

## Affects

- bd-joh9: Gemini Analysis Action Items (top-level epic)
- bd-9j1q: Backlog Refactor: 2026 Development Phases (parent roadmap)
- bd-joh9.1, bd-joh9.2, bd-joh9.3: Sub-epics for scripting, experiment, data plane
title: Gemini analysis items as cross-cutting sibling epic under bd-9j1q

notes:
## Protocol Details (Manual pp 18-19, 26-27)

### Commands
- `c1` - Scan current curve for Motor 1
- `c2` - Scan current curve for Motor 2

### Format
- **Request**: `A c1` or `A c2`
- **Response**: `A CS [522 bytes of data]`

### Data Structure
- Total: 522 bytes (87 data points × 6 bytes each)
- Each data point: 2 bytes frequency + 2 bytes fwd current + 2 bytes bwd current
- Frequency range: 70-120 kHz (87 points at ~575 Hz intervals)

### Current Conversion
```
current_amps = adc_value / 1866.0
```

### Timing
- Takes approximately 12 seconds to complete scan
- Motor moves during scan - ensure safe clearance

### Response Code
- `CS` - Current Scan data

### Implementation Notes
- Useful for motor diagnostics and resonance analysis
- Returns raw ADC values, convert with 1866 ADC = 1 Amp
- Parse as binary data after ASCII command prefix
- Consider async streaming or timeout handling for 12s operation
title: Current Curve Scan (c1/c2)

close_reason: Created SECURITY.md with comprehensive plugin security trust model documentation. Covers: in-process execution risks, memory/filesystem/network access implications, administrator recommendations (trusted sources, code review, directory permissions, --no-plugins usage), developer guidelines, and network security. Note: The --no-plugins CLI flag and enhanced startup logging mentioned in the issue are code changes that should be tracked as separate tasks.
description:
# Task: Document Plugin Security Trust Model

## Problem
The plugin system (`daq-plugin-api`) uses `abi_stable` for ABI safety but loads plugins directly into the host process space with **zero isolation**:
- A crash in a plugin will crash the daemon
- Plugins can call `exit()`, segfault, or corrupt daemon memory
- No sandboxing exists

This is an acceptable trade-off for performance but MUST be documented.

## Requirements

### 1. Update SECURITY.md
Add a new section "Plugin Security Model":
```markdown
## Plugin Security Model

**WARNING: Plugins are trusted code.**

Plugins loaded by rust-daq run in the same process space as the daemon with full access to:
- All daemon memory
- All file system access the daemon has
- Network access
- Hardware devices

### Implications
- A malicious or buggy plugin can crash the daemon
- Only install plugins from trusted sources
- Plugins should only be installed by system administrators
- Plugin directory should have restricted permissions (e.g., `/usr/lib/daq/plugins` owned by root)

### Recommendations
- Review plugin source code before installation
- Use `--no-plugins` flag in high-security environments
- Monitor daemon logs for plugin load messages
```

### 2. Add startup logging
Ensure daemon logs all loaded plugins loudly at startup:
```
INFO  Loading plugin: my-plugin v1.0.0 from /usr/lib/daq/plugins/libmy_plugin.so
WARN  Plugin security: Plugins run with full daemon privileges. See SECURITY.md
```

### 3. Add `--no-plugins` CLI flag
Add a safe mode flag to disable plugin loading entirely.

## Files to Modify
- `SECURITY.md` (create if not exists)
- `crates/daq-bin/src/main.rs` (CLI flag)
- `crates/daq-plugin-api/src/manager.rs` (logging)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] SECURITY.md documents plugin trust model
- [ ] Daemon logs all loaded plugins at startup
- [ ] `--no-plugins` flag available
- [ ] Plugin directory permissions documented
title: Document plugin security trust model

notes:
## Problem
The register_primary_output() API was added to FrameProducer trait (bd-0dax.5) but the actual frame loops don't send frames through primary_tx yet. Frames only go through the broadcast channel (frame_tx).

## Files to Update
1. crates/daq-driver-pvcam/src/components/acquisition.rs (~line 2223)
   - After creating the frame, check if primary_tx is Some
   - Send LoanedFrame through primary_tx instead of/in addition to broadcast

2. crates/daq-hardware/src/drivers/mock.rs (~line 553)
   - Same pattern for MockCamera

## Implementation
The frame loop should:
1. Acquire frame from pool (already done)
2. Run taps/observers (already done)
3. If primary_tx is set, send LoanedFrame ownership through it
4. If not set, use broadcast channel as fallback

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] PVCAM frame loop sends through primary_tx when registered
- [ ] MockCamera frame loop sends through primary_tx when registered
- [ ] Tests can use register_primary_output() to receive frames
- [ ] Existing broadcast-based tests continue to work

description:
# Clean Up daq-hardware

## Purpose
Remove all driver code from daq-hardware, leaving only:
- Capability trait re-exports
- DriverBuilder trait
- DeviceRegistry
- YAML Plugin system

## Files to Remove
```
crates/daq-hardware/src/drivers/
├── mock.rs           → moved to daq-driver-mock
├── ell14.rs          → moved to daq-driver-thorlabs
├── ell14_polling.rs  → moved to daq-driver-thorlabs
├── esp300.rs         → moved to daq-driver-newport
├── newport_1830c.rs  → moved to daq-driver-newport
├── maitai.rs         → moved to daq-driver-spectra-physics
└── mod.rs            → DELETE (no more drivers module)
```

## Cargo.toml Changes
```toml
# REMOVE these dependencies:
# tokio-serial = ...   # Only needed by drivers
# daq-driver-pvcam = ...
# daq-driver-comedi = ...

# KEEP these:
inventory = "0.3"
daq-core = { path = "../daq-core" }
futures = "0.3"
toml = "0.8"
serde = { version = "1", features = ["derive"] }
# ... other core deps

# REMOVE these features:
# thorlabs, newport, spectra_physics, pvcam, comedi
# (these are now in daq-drivers metacrate)
```

## lib.rs Changes
```rust
// REMOVE:
// pub mod drivers;
// #[cfg(feature = "thorlabs")]
// pub use drivers::ell14;
// etc.

// KEEP:
pub mod capabilities;
pub mod registry;
pub mod driver_builder;
pub mod plugin;  // YAML plugin system
```

## Registry Changes
1. Remove `DriverType` enum entirely (or keep minimal for transition)
2. Remove `ell14_shared_ports` field
3. Remove all feature-gated driver imports
4. Keep only `instantiate_dynamic()` path

## Migration Steps
1. Verify all driver crates compile standalone
2. Delete drivers/ directory
3. Update lib.rs exports
4. Clean Cargo.toml dependencies
5. Clean Cargo.toml features
6. Update registry.rs (remove DriverType if ready)
7. Run `cargo check -p daq-hardware`
8. Run all tests

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] No driver code in daq-hardware
- [ ] `cargo check -p daq-hardware` passes with minimal deps
- [ ] daq-hardware compiles without any driver features
- [ ] tokio-serial not a dependency of daq-hardware
- [ ] All functionality works via daq-drivers
title: Phase 9: Clean up daq-hardware (remove driver code)

close_reason: Implemented missing protocol features for ELL14.
notes:
## Protocol Details (Manual pp 15-16, 23-24)

### Commands
- `f1` / `b1` - Get/Set Motor 1 forward/backward periods
- `f2` / `b2` - Get/Set Motor 2 forward/backward periods

### Format
- **Get**: `A f1` → Response: `A GF XXXXXXXX` (8 hex chars: fwd period + bwd period)
- **Set**: `A f1 XXXX` where XXXX is period with MSB set to '8' (e.g., `8XXX`)
- Send `8FFF` to restore factory default periods

### Period Calculation
```
Period = 14740000 / frequency_hz
```

### Response Codes
- `GF` - Motor 1 periods (Get Forward)
- `GB` - Motor 2 periods (Get Backward)

### Implementation Notes
- Period values have MSB '8' prefix when setting
- Response contains both forward and backward periods concatenated
- Each period is 4 hex chars (16-bit value)
- ELL14 has 2 motors, so need f1/b1 AND f2/b2
title: Motor Period Commands (f1/b1/f2/b2)
